steps.title: Understand what Text 2 recommends
steps.content:
Text 1 describes people hiding their preferences to appear easygoing. Text 2 presents research that directly refutes this: not stating preferences does not make you more likable, makes decisions harder for others, and lowers their satisfaction with the outcome. The author of Text 2 would recommend whatever the research proves works — stating your preference.

Now check each choice against that conclusion:
  • Choice A — 'Cancel the plan to have lunch together.': Canceling avoids the decision entirely. Text 2 says nothing about canceling plans; it addresses how to make joint decisions effectively. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Ask where the friend typically likes to eat.': Asking the friend's preference still avoids stating your own. Text 2 identifies not revealing your preference as the problematic behavior. ✗
  • Choice C — 'State a preference about where to eat.': Stating your preference is exactly what Text 2's research supports — it doesn't reduce likability and makes decisions easier for the other person. ✓
  • Choice D — 'Change the subject to talk about something else.': Changing the subject sidesteps the decision entirely, which contradicts Text 2's implicit advice to engage directly. ✗

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.content: Text 2's research shows that stating a preference is the approach that avoids all the negative outcomes described. That matches choice C — C is correct.
